perturbation.original_text: The Company shall ensure that all goods and services provided by the Company, under or in association with any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks, shall (i) be substantially the same as or greater than the quality of goods and services provided under such Seller Licensed Trademarks immediately prior to the Effective Date and (ii) not be associated with any goods or services, including any activities, that are reasonably likely to have an adverse effect on (A) the image or reputation of any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks or (B) Seller's right, title or interest in and to, any of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will try to ensure that goods and services provided by the Company, under or in association with any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks, are generally similar in quality to the goods and services previously provided. It is the intention that goods and services (i) not be significantly inferior and (ii) are not knowingly associated with activities that could harm the image or reputation of any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks.
perturbation.explanation: The modification from 'shall ensure' to 'will try to ensure' introduces ambiguity. Additionally, replacing 'substantially the same as or greater than' with 'generally similar' and adding qualifiers like 'not significantly inferior' and 'not knowingly associated' weakens the quality control obligation, making it subjective and difficult to enforce. This creates a loophole where the Company could argue that its efforts were sufficient, even if the quality declines.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Lanham Act - Trademark Infringement and Quality Control
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies for Infringement; Profits; Damages; Costs; Attorney Fees
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act requires trademark owners to maintain control over the quality of goods and services associated with their trademarks to prevent consumer deception. By weakening the quality control obligations to 'generally similar' and introducing subjective terms, the modified text fails to ensure consistent quality, potentially deceiving consumers and harming the trademark's reputation, conflicting with the core purpose of the Lanham Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant- (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any g
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contract's quality control obligations, potentially conflicting with trademark law's intent to prevent consumer deception. The change from 'shall ensure' to 'will try to ensure', along with the replacement of 'substantially the same as or greater than' with 'generally similar', introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of quality standards. While the provided snippets from 15 U.S. Code § 1114 focus on infringement, the law explanation highlights the broader requirement to maintain quality to avoid misleading consumers, which is undermined by the ambiguous language in the modified contract. The shift to subjective terms such as 'not significantly inferior' and 'not knowingly associated' further weakens the original obligation, creating a loophole where the company could argue its efforts were sufficient even if the quality declined and consumers were deceived. This contradiction falls under Ambiguity Legal Contradiction as the introduction of ambiguous language makes the original obligation difficult to enforce, potentially resulting in non-compliance with regulatory requirements.

perturbation.original_text: In addition to the obligations set forth in Section 6.3, and notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, the Company shall comply with all obligations applicable to Arizona and its Affiliates under the Trademark License Agreement including, for the avoidance of doubt, any obligations with respect to reporting Complaints (as defined in the Trademark License Agreement), which reports the Company shall provide to Arizona, and quality control and standards, and Licensor Competitors (as defined in the Trademark License Agreement), and neither Party shall undertake any act that would constitute a breach or a basis for termination under the Trademark License Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition to the obligations set forth in Section 6.3, and notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, the Company should endeavor to meet obligations applicable to Arizona and its Affiliates under the Trademark License Agreement. The Company will forward relevant reports related to major Complaints (as defined in the Trademark License Agreement) to Arizona when reasonably practical. Each party should avoid actions that clearly constitute a breach of the Trademark License Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposed a strict obligation to comply with all obligations under the Trademark License Agreement. The modified text replaces 'shall comply' with 'should endeavor to meet', and 'the Company shall provide' with 'will forward relevant reports related to major Complaints... when reasonably practical', both of which significantly weaken the obligation. Removing the explicit mention of 'quality control and standards, and Licensor Competitors' also creates ambiguity about the scope of compliance. The phrase 'clearly constitute a breach' introduces further subjectivity, making it harder to determine a violation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract and Trademark License Agreement Enforcement
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware law (as the contract is governed by Delaware law) regarding breach of contract and specific performance
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Delaware contract law, a material breach allows the non-breaching party to seek remedies such as specific performance. By changing 'shall comply' to 'should endeavor to meet' and introducing subjective qualifiers to the reporting requirement, the modification makes it significantly more difficult to prove a material breach of the intellectual property agreement related to adherence to the underlying Trademark License Agreement, thus hindering enforcement of obligations and potentially harming the trademark licensor's rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Chapter 27 Authenticated PDF CONTRACTS Subchapter I. General Provisions Subchapter II. Statute of Frauds and Perjuries Subchapter III. Equipment Dealer Contracts Subchapter IV. Consumer Contracts
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the original obligation to comply with the Trademark License Agreement, creating an ambiguous legal obligation. The original text uses 'shall comply,' which is a strict obligation, while the modified text uses 'should endeavor to meet,' which is a much weaker, non-committal phrase. Additionally, the original text states 'the Company shall provide,' whereas the changed text says 'will forward relevant reports related to major Complaints... when reasonably practical,' introducing subjectivity. This ambiguity makes it difficult to enforce the obligations and could lead to disputes over compliance, satisfying the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Arizona will reasonably cooperate with the Company to obtain, record, and perfect title to, and provide all necessary evidence of the Company's ownership of, the Arizona Assigned IP, including the execution of (i) a Patent Assignment in the form of the attached Exhibit B, and (ii) a Trademark Assignment in the form of the attached Exhibit C.
perturbation.changed_text: Arizona will consider cooperating with the Company, as it deems appropriate, to assist in recording and perfecting title to the Arizona Assigned IP. This consideration may include the execution of a Patent Assignment substantially similar to Exhibit B and a Trademark Assignment generally reflecting Exhibit C.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces significant ambiguity by replacing 'will reasonably cooperate' with 'will consider cooperating, as it deems appropriate,' making the cooperation discretionary. The original language clearly obligates Arizona to provide 'all necessary evidence' and execute specific assignments. The modified language only commits to 'considering' cooperation and using documents that are 'substantially similar' or 'generally reflecting' the exhibits, allowing Arizona to deviate from the required actions and potentially hindering the Company's ability to secure its ownership rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - Duty of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware's adoption of the UCC imposes a duty of good faith in the performance of contracts. By making its cooperation discretionary ('as it deems appropriate') and weakening the obligation to provide necessary evidence and execute specific assignments, Arizona may be violating its duty of good faith, frustrating the purpose of the agreement and denying the Company the benefit of its bargain. A good faith standard generally means a reasonable and honest effort to meet the obligations of the contract, not a discretionary decision of whether or not to cooperate.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity by changing 'will reasonably cooperate' to 'will consider cooperating, as it deems appropriate.' This weakens Arizona's obligation to assist the Company in securing its ownership rights, potentially violating the duty of good faith as described in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC implications in Delaware Code Title 6, mentions that 'the obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement.' By making cooperation discretionary, Arizona could be seen as attempting to disclaim or at least weaken its obligation to act in good faith, thereby creating a legal contradiction.
